name: Katha Upanishad
summary: The Katha Upanishad presents the dialogue between young Nachiketa and Yama, the god of death. Nachiketa asks for the secret of death and immortality. Yama reveals the nature of the Atman — smaller than the smallest, greater than the greatest — and the path to liberation through discrimination and self-control.

verses.sanskrit:
उशन् ह वै वाजश्रवसः सर्ववेदसं ददौ |
तस्य ह नचिकेता नाम पुत्र आस ||१.१.१||
verses.transliteration:
uśan ha vai vājaśravasaḥ sarvavedasaṃ dadau |
tasya ha naciketā nāma putra āsa ||1.1.1||
verses.english_translation: Vajashravasa, desiring the fruit of the sacrifice, gave away all his possessions. He had a son named Nachiketa.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: वाजश्रवसः — वाजश्रवसः पुत्रः — सर्ववेदसं सर्वस्वं ददौ — विश्वजिद्यागे।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Vajashravasa, son of Vajashravas, gave away all his wealth in the Vishvajit sacrifice, desiring its fruits. His son Nachiketa observed this.

verses.sanskrit: स होवाच पितरं तत कस्मै मां दास्यसीति | द्वितीयं तृतीयं | तं होवाच मृत्यवे त्वा ददामीति ||१.१.२||
verses.transliteration: sa hovāca pitaraṃ tata kasmai māṃ dāsyasīti | dvitīyaṃ tṛtīyaṃ | taṃ hovāca mṛtyave tvā dadāmīti ||1.1.2||

word_meanings.meaning: Father, to whom will you give me?

verses.english_translation: He said to his father: 'Father, to whom will you give me?' A second and third time. He said to him: 'I give you to Death.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नचिकेताः पितरम् अपृच्छत् — कस्मै माम् दास्यसि। पिता क्रुद्धः — मृत्यवे त्वा ददामि — इत्युक्तवान्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa, seeing his father give away old and barren cows, asked three times to whom he would give his son. The father, irritated, said 'I give you to Death.' Nachiketa, taking his father's word seriously, went to Yama's abode.

verses.english_translation: Verse 3 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.

verses.sanskrit: नचिकेता उवाच | अनशनन्त्रिरात्रमतिथिर्ब्राह्मणो गृहे | तस्यानशनेन किं स्यादिति चिन्तयन् ||१.१.७||
verses.transliteration: naciketā uvāca | anaśnan trirātram atithir brāhmaṇo gṛhe | tasyānaśanena kiṃ syād iti cintayan ||1.1.7||
verses.english_translation: Nachiketa said: 'A brahmin guest fasting for three nights in a house — what happens by his fasting?'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नचिकेताः त्रिरात्रम् अनशन् — यमस्य गृहे उपवासम् अकरोत्। ब्राह्मणस्य अतिथेः अनशनेन गृहस्थस्य पापम् भवति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa waited at Yama's door for three nights without food. When Yama returned, he was told that a brahmin guest had been fasting — which brings harm to the host. Yama offered three boons to compensate.

verses.sanskrit: तिस्रो रात्रीर्यदवात्सीर्गृहे मेऽनश्नन् ब्रह्मन् | नमस्तेऽस्तु ब्रह्मन् स्वस्ति मेऽस्तु | तस्मात्प्रति त्रीन् वरान् वृणीष्व ||१.१.९||
verses.transliteration: tisro rātrīr yad avātsīr gṛhe me'naśnan brahman | namas te'stu brahman svasti me'stu | tasmāt prati trīn varān vṛṇīṣva ||1.1.9||
verses.english_translation: You stayed three nights in my house without eating, O brahmin. Salutation to you, O brahmin. May it be well with me. Therefore choose three boons.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: यमः नचिकेतसम् अब्रवीत् — त्रिरात्रम् अनश्नन् अवात्सीः — तस्मात् त्रीन् वरान् वृणीष्व।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Yama, the lord of death, honored Nachiketa and offered three boons as compensation for the three nights of fasting. This shows that even Yama respects a sincere brahmin seeker.

verses.sanskrit: शान्तसंकल्पः सुमना यथा स्याद्वीतमन्युर्गौतमो माभि मृत्यो | त्वत्प्रसृष्टं माभिवदेत्प्रतीत | एतत्त्रयाणां प्रथमं वरं वृणे ||१.१.१०||
verses.transliteration: śāntasaṃkalpaḥ sumanā yathā syād vītamanyur gautamo mābhi mṛtyo | tvat prasṛṣṭaṃ mābhi vadet pratīta | etat trayāṇāṃ prathamaṃ varaṃ vṛṇe ||1.1.10||

word_meanings.meaning: of peaceful mind and good disposition

verses.english_translation: May my father Gautama be of peaceful mind and good disposition, free from anger toward me, O Death. May he recognize me when released by you. This I choose as the first of the three boons.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रथमः वरः — पितुः शान्तिः। नचिकेताः पितरम् प्रथमम् स्मरति — पितृभक्तिम् दर्शयति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa's first boon is for his father's peace of mind. This shows Nachiketa's character — even before asking about immortality, he thinks of his father's welfare. Yama grants this boon immediately.

verses.sanskrit: स्वर्गे लोके न भयं किञ्चनास्ति न तत्र त्वं न जरया बिभेति | उभे तीर्त्वाऽशनायापिपासे शोकातिगो मोदते स्वर्गलोके ||१.१.१२||
verses.transliteration: svarge loke na bhayaṃ kiñcanāsti na tatra tvaṃ na jarayā bibheti | ubhe tīrtvā'śanāyāpipāse śokātigo modate svargaloke ||1.1.12||

word_meanings.meaning: in the heavenly world there is no fear

verses.english_translation: In the heavenly world there is no fear whatsoever. You are not there, nor does one fear old age. Having crossed both hunger and thirst, beyond grief, one rejoices in the heavenly world.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: स्वर्गे — देवलोके — भयम् नास्ति। जरा नास्ति। क्षुत्पिपासे नास्ते। शोकः नास्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa describes the heavenly world as free from fear, old age, hunger, thirst, and grief. He asks for the Nachiketa fire (the ritual that leads to heaven) as his second boon. Yama grants this and names the fire after Nachiketa.

verses.sanskrit: येयं प्रेते विचिकित्सा मनुष्येऽस्तीत्येके नायमस्तीति चैके | एतद्विद्यामनुशिष्टस्त्वयाऽहं वराणामेष वरस्तृतीयः ||१.१.२०||
verses.transliteration: yeyaṃ prete vicikitsā manuṣye'stīty eke nāyam astīti caike | etad vidyām anuśiṣṭas tvayā'haṃ varāṇām eṣa varas tṛtīyaḥ ||1.1.20||

word_meanings.meaning: others say he does not exist

verses.english_translation: There is this doubt about a person who has died — some say 'he exists,' others say 'he does not exist.' I wish to be taught this by you. This is the third of the boons.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रेते — मृते — विचिकित्सा — संशयः। अस्ति इति — आत्मा अस्ति। न अस्ति इति — आत्मा नास्ति। एतत् विद्याम् — इदम् ज्ञातुम् इच्छामि।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa's third boon is the most profound — the secret of death and the existence of the Self after death. This is the central question of the Katha Upanishad. Yama tries to dissuade Nachiketa with wealth and pleasures, but Nachiketa insists.

verses.sanskrit: न वित्तेन तर्पणीयो मनुष्यो लप्स्यामहे वित्तमद्राक्ष्म चेत्त्वा | जीविष्यामो यावदीशिष्यसि त्वं वरस्तु मे वरणीयः स एव ||१.१.२७||
verses.transliteration: na vittena tarpaṇīyo manuṣyo lapsyāmahe vittam adrākṣma cet tvā | jīviṣyāmo yāvad īśiṣyasi tvaṃ varas tu me varaṇīyaḥ sa eva ||1.1.27||

word_meanings.meaning: a person cannot be satisfied by wealth

verses.english_translation: A person cannot be satisfied by wealth. We shall obtain wealth when we have seen you. We shall live as long as you rule. But the boon to be chosen by me is that very one.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न वित्तेन तर्पणीयः — धनेन मनुष्यः न तृप्यति। वरः मे वरणीयः स एव — मृत्युरहस्यम् एव वृणे।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa refuses all of Yama's temptations — wealth, women, kingdoms, long life. He says a person cannot be satisfied by wealth. The only boon worth choosing is the knowledge of the Self. This shows Nachiketa's supreme vairagya (dispassion).

verses.sanskrit: श्रेयश्च प्रेयश्च मनुष्यमेतः | तौ सम्परीत्य विविनक्ति धीरः | श्रेयो हि धीरोऽभि प्रेयसो वृणीते | प्रेयो मन्दो योगक्षेमाद्वृणीते ||१.२.२||
verses.transliteration: śreyaś ca preyaś ca manuṣyam etaḥ | tau samparītya vivinakti dhīraḥ | śreyo hi dhīro'bhi preyaso vṛṇīte | preyo mando yogakṣemād vṛṇīte ||1.2.2||
verses.english_translation: Both the good and the pleasant approach a person. The wise one examines them and discriminates. The wise one chooses the good over the pleasant. The foolish one chooses the pleasant for the sake of comfort.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: श्रेयः — मोक्षसाधनम्। प्रेयः — भोगसाधनम्। धीरः — विवेकी — श्रेयः वृणीते। मन्दः — अविवेकी — प्रेयः वृणीते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Shreyas is what leads to the highest good (liberation); preyas is what is merely pleasant (sense enjoyment). The wise discriminate between them and choose the good. The foolish choose the pleasant. Nachiketa chose shreyas — the knowledge of the Self.

verses.sanskrit: आत्मानं रथिनं विद्धि शरीरं रथमेव तु | बुद्धिं तु सारथिं विद्धि मनः प्रग्रहमेव च ||१.३.३||
verses.transliteration: ātmānaṃ rathinaṃ viddhi śarīraṃ ratham eva tu | buddhiṃ tu sārathiṃ viddhi manaḥ pragraham eva ca ||1.3.3||

word_meanings.meaning: know the Self as the rider of the chariot

verses.english_translation: Know the Self as the rider of the chariot, the body as the chariot, the intellect as the charioteer, and the mind as the reins.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: आत्मा — रथी — भोक्ता। शरीरम् — रथः — भोगसाधनम्। बुद्धिः — सारथिः — निर्णायिका। मनः — प्रग्रहः — संकल्पविकल्पात्मकम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: This famous chariot analogy describes the relationship between the Self, body, intellect, mind, and senses. The Self is the passenger; the body is the vehicle; the intellect is the driver; the mind is the reins; the senses are the horses; the sense objects are the roads.

verses.transliteration: indriyāṇi hayān āhur viṣayāṃs teṣu gocarān | ātmendriyamanoyuktaṃ bhoktety āhur manīṣiṇaḥ ||1.3.4||

word_meanings.meaning: the Self joined with senses and mind

verses.english_translation: The senses are called the horses; the sense objects are their roads. The Self joined with the senses and mind — the wise call it the enjoyer.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: इन्द्रियाणि — हयाः — विषयेषु धावन्ति। विषयाः — गोचराः — मार्गाः। आत्मा इन्द्रियमनोयुक्तः — भोक्ता — संसारी।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The senses are like horses that run toward their objects. The sense objects are the roads they travel. The Self, when identified with the senses and mind, becomes the enjoyer — the bound individual soul in samsara.

verses.sanskrit: यस्त्वविज्ञानवान् भवत्ययुक्तेन मनसा सदा | तस्येन्द्रियाण्यवश्यानि दुष्टाश्वा इव सारथेः ||१.३.५||
verses.transliteration: yas tv avijñānavān bhavaty ayuktena manasā sadā | tasyendriyāṇy avaśyāni duṣṭāśvā iva sārtheḥ ||1.3.5||
verses.english_translation: One who is without discrimination, with always uncontrolled mind — his senses are uncontrolled, like vicious horses of a charioteer.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अविज्ञानवान् — विवेकरहितः — अयुक्तेन मनसा — असंयतचित्तेन — तस्य इन्द्रियाणि अवश्यानि — दुष्टाश्वाः इव।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Without discrimination (viveka) and an uncontrolled mind, the senses run wild like vicious horses. Such a person is dragged through samsara. The solution is to develop discrimination and control the mind through the reins of the intellect.

verses.sanskrit: तद्विष्णोः परमं पदं सदा पश्यन्ति सूरयः | दिवीव चक्षुरातातम् ||१.३.९||
verses.transliteration: tad viṣṇoḥ paramaṃ padaṃ sadā paśyanti sūrayaḥ | divīva cakṣur ātatam ||1.3.9||

word_meanings.meaning: like an eye spread across the sky

verses.english_translation: The wise always see the supreme abode of Vishnu, spread out like an eye across the sky.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: विष्णोः परमम् पदम् — परब्रह्मणः उत्तमम् स्थानम् — सूरयः — ज्ञानिनः — सदा पश्यन्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The supreme abode of Vishnu (Brahman) is always seen by the wise — not with physical eyes but with the eye of knowledge. It is spread everywhere like the sky, and the wise perceive it in all things.

verses.sanskrit: न जायते म्रियते वा विपश्चिन्नायं कुतश्चिन्न बभूव कश्चित् | अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ||१.२.१८||
verses.transliteration: na jāyate mriyate vā vipaścin nāyaṃ kutaścin na babhūva kaścit | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||1.2.18||

word_meanings.meaning: is not killed when the body is killed

verses.english_translation: The wise one is not born, nor does it die. It did not come from anywhere, nor did it become anything. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient — it is not killed when the body is killed.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न जायते — उत्पत्तिरहितः। न म्रियते — विनाशरहितः। अजः — जन्मरहितः। नित्यः — कालत्रयेऽपि अस्ति। न हन्यते — शरीरनाशेऽपि आत्मा न नश्यति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is beyond birth and death. It was not born, it does not die, it has no origin and no end. It is unborn, eternal, ancient, and everlasting. When the body is destroyed, the Self is not destroyed — for the Self is not the body. This verse also appears in the Bhagavad Gita (2.20).

verses.sanskrit: अङ्गुष्ठमात्रः पुरुषो मध्य आत्मनि तिष्ठति | ईशानो भूतभव्यस्य न ततो विजुगुप्सते ||२.१.१२||
verses.transliteration: aṅguṣṭhamātraḥ puruṣo madhya ātmani tiṣṭhati | īśāno bhūtabhaviṣyasya na tato vijugupsate ||2.1.12||

word_meanings.meaning: the Person of the size of a thumb
stands in the middle of the Self
lord of the past and future

verses.english_translation: The Person of the size of a thumb stands in the middle of the Self, lord of the past and future. One does not shrink away from that.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अङ्गुष्ठमात्रः — हृदयाकाशे अङ्गुष्ठपरिमाणः — उपाधिना। वास्तवतः अनन्तः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is described as thumb-sized only in relation to its location in the heart — not as its actual size. In reality, the Self is infinite. The thumb-sized description is a meditation aid to locate the Self in the heart.

verses.sanskrit: अङ्गुष्ठमात्रः पुरुषो ज्योतिरिवाधूमकः | ईशानो भूतभव्यस्य स एवाद्य स उ श्वः ||२.१.१३||
verses.transliteration: aṅguṣṭhamātraḥ puruṣo jyotir ivādhūmakaḥ | īśāno bhūtabhaviṣyasya sa evādya sa u śvaḥ ||2.1.13||

word_meanings.word: स एव अद्य स उ श्वः
word_meanings.meaning: he is the same today and tomorrow

verses.english_translation: The Person of the size of a thumb is like a smokeless flame, lord of the past and future. He is the same today and tomorrow.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: ज्योतिः इव अधूमकः — धूमरहितः दीपः इव — शुद्धप्रकाशः। स एव अद्य — कालत्रयेऽपि अपरिवर्तनीयः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is like a smokeless flame — pure light without any impurity. It is the lord of past and future — it transcends time. It is the same today and tomorrow — unchanging, eternal, beyond all modification.

verses.sanskrit: नैव वाचा न मनसा प्राप्तुं शक्यो न चक्षुषा | अस्तीति ब्रुवतोऽन्यत्र कथं तदुपलभ्यते ||२.३.१२||
verses.transliteration: naiva vācā na manasā prāptuṃ śakyo na cakṣuṣā | astīti bruvato'nyatra kathaṃ tad upalabhyate ||2.3.12||
verses.english_translation: It cannot be attained by speech, nor by mind, nor by the eye. How is it perceived except by one who says 'it exists'?
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न वाचा — वाचा न गृह्यते। न मनसा — मनसा न ज्ञायते। अस्ति इति ब्रुवतः — श्रद्धावतः — उपलभ्यते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self cannot be grasped by speech, mind, or eye. It is perceived only by one who has faith that it exists — who approaches it with shraddha (faith) and the guidance of a teacher. The Self reveals itself to the sincere seeker.

verses.transliteration: astīty evopalabdhavyas tattvabhāvena cobhayoḥ | astīty evopalabdhasya tattvabhāvaḥ prasīdati ||2.3.13||
verses.english_translation: It is to be perceived as 'it exists' and in its true nature — of both. For one who has perceived it as 'it exists,' its true nature becomes clear.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अस्ति इति — प्रथमम् आत्मा अस्ति इति श्रद्धा। ततः तत्त्वभावः — स्वरूपज्ञानम् — प्रसीदति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The first step is faith that the Self exists (astita). The second step is knowing its true nature (tattvabhava). For one who has the first — faith in the Self's existence — the second naturally follows. Faith opens the door to direct knowledge.

verses.sanskrit: यच्छेद्वाङ्मनसी प्राज्ञस्तद्यच्छेज्ज्ञान आत्मनि | ज्ञानमात्मनि महति नियच्छेत्तद्यच्छेच्छान्त आत्मनि ||१.३.१३||
verses.transliteration: yacched vāṅ manasī prājñas tad yacchej jñāna ātmani | jñānam ātmani mahati niyacchet tad yacchec chānta ātmani ||1.3.13||

word_meanings.meaning: let him restrain speech in mind
let him restrain mind in the knowing Self

verses.english_translation: Let the wise restrain speech in mind, mind in the knowing Self, the knowing Self in the great Self, and that in the peaceful Self.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: वाक् मनसि — वाचम् मनसि लयं नयेत्। मनः ज्ञाने — मनः बुद्धौ। ज्ञानम् महति — बुद्धिम् महत्तत्त्वे। तत् शान्ते — शान्ते ब्रह्मणि।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: This describes the yogic process of withdrawal (pratyahara). Speech is dissolved into mind, mind into intellect, intellect into the great Self (mahat), and that into the peaceful Brahman. This is the path of meditation leading to samadhi.

verses.sanskrit: उत्तिष्ठत जाग्रत प्राप्य वरान्निबोधत | क्षुरस्य धारा निशिता दुरत्यया दुर्गं पथस्तत्कवयो वदन्ति ||१.३.१४||
verses.transliteration: uttiṣṭhata jāgrata prāpya varān nibodhata | kṣurasya dhārā niśitā duratyayā durgaṃ pathas tat kavayo vadanti ||1.3.14||
verses.english_translation: Arise, awake! Having reached the great, learn. The edge of a razor is sharp and hard to cross — the wise say the path is difficult.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: उत्तिष्ठत — आलस्यम् त्यज। जाग्रत — अज्ञाननिद्राम् त्यज। क्षुरस्य धारा — तीक्ष्णा — दुरत्यया — दुःखेन अतिक्रम्यते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Arise from the sleep of ignorance! Awake to the knowledge of the Self! The path of self-knowledge is like the edge of a razor — sharp and difficult to walk. But having reached a great teacher, learn from him. This famous verse is a call to spiritual urgency.

verses.sanskrit: न जायते म्रियते वा कदाचिन्नायं भूत्वा भविता वा न भूयः | अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ||१.२.१८||
verses.transliteration: na jāyate mriyate vā kadācin nāyaṃ bhūtvā bhaviṣyati vā na bhūyaḥ | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||1.2.18||

word_meanings.meaning: having been, will not be again
not killed when the body is killed

verses.english_translation: It is never born, nor does it ever die. Having come into being, it will not cease to be. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient — it is not killed when the body is killed.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न जायते — उत्पत्तिरहितः। न म्रियते — विनाशरहितः। अजः — जन्मरहितः। नित्यः — कालत्रयेऽपि अस्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is beyond birth and death. It was not born, it does not die, it has no origin and no end. When the body is destroyed, the Self is not destroyed — for the Self is not the body. This verse also appears in the Bhagavad Gita (2.20) — one of the most quoted verses in all of Sanskrit literature.

verses.sanskrit:
अणोरणीयान् महतो महीयानात्मास्य जन्तोर्निहितो गुहायाम् |
तमक्रतुः पश्यति वीतशोको धातुप्रसादान्महिमानमात्मनः ||१.२.२०||
verses.transliteration:
aṇor aṇīyān mahato mahīyān ātmāsya jantor nihito guhāyām |
tam akratuḥ paśyati vītaśoko dhātuprasādān mahimānam ātmanaḥ ||1.2.20||

word_meanings.meaning: hidden in the cave of the heart
by the grace of the Creator

verses.english_translation: Smaller than the smallest, greater than the greatest, the Self is hidden in the heart of this creature. One free from desire and grief, by the grace of the Creator, sees the glory of the Self.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अणोः अणीयान् — परमाणोरपि सूक्ष्मतरः। महतः महीयान् — महत्तत्त्वादपि महत्तरः। धातुप्रसादात् — ईश्वरानुग्रहात्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is subtler than the subtlest atom and vaster than the vastest. It is hidden in the cave of the heart. Only one who is free from desire and sorrow, by the grace of the Lord, can perceive the glory of the Self. Grace is essential — knowledge alone is not enough without divine grace.

verses.sanskrit: इन्द्रियेभ्यः परा ह्यर्था अर्थेभ्यश्च परं मनः | मनसस्तु परा बुद्धिर्बुद्धेरात्मा महान्परः ||१.३.१०||
verses.transliteration: indriyebhyaḥ parā hy arthā arthebhyaś ca paraṃ manaḥ | manasas tu parā buddhir buddher ātmā mahān paraḥ ||1.3.10||

word_meanings.meaning: beyond the senses are the objects
beyond the objects is the mind
beyond the mind is the intellect
beyond the intellect is the great Self

verses.english_translation: Beyond the senses are the objects; beyond the objects is the mind; beyond the mind is the intellect; beyond the intellect is the great Self.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: इन्द्रियेभ्यः परा — इन्द्रियाणाम् विषयाः सूक्ष्मतराः। अर्थेभ्यः परम् मनः — विषयेभ्यः मनः सूक्ष्मतरम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: This describes the hierarchy of reality from gross to subtle. The senses are gross; their objects are subtler; the mind is subtler still; the intellect is subtler than the mind; and the great Self (mahat atman) is the subtlest of all. This is the map of consciousness used in Samkhya and Vedanta.

verses.sanskrit: महतः परमव्यक्तमव्यक्तात्पुरुषः परः | पुरुषान्न परं किञ्चित्सा काष्ठा सा परा गतिः ||१.३.११||
verses.transliteration: mahataḥ param avyaktam avyaktāt puruṣaḥ paraḥ | puruṣān na paraṃ kiñcit sā kāṣṭhā sā parā gatiḥ ||1.3.11||

word_meanings.meaning: beyond the great is the unmanifest
beyond the unmanifest is the Purusha
beyond the Purusha there is nothing
that is the end, that is the highest goal

verses.english_translation: Beyond the great is the unmanifest; beyond the unmanifest is the Purusha. Beyond the Purusha there is nothing. That is the end; that is the highest goal.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: महतः परम् — महत्तत्त्वात् परम् — अव्यक्तम् — प्रकृतिः। अव्यक्तात् परः — पुरुषः — परब्रह्म। पुरुषात् परम् — नास्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Beyond the great Self is the unmanifest (avyakta/Prakriti); beyond that is the Purusha (Brahman). Beyond Brahman there is nothing — it is the ultimate end, the highest goal. All spiritual seeking culminates in the realization of Brahman.

verses.sanskrit: एष सर्वेषु भूतेषु गूढोऽत्मा न प्रकाशते | दृश्यते त्वग्र्यया बुद्ध्या सूक्ष्मया सूक्ष्मदर्शिभिः ||१.३.१२||
verses.transliteration: eṣa sarveṣu bhūteṣu gūḍho'tmā na prakāśate | dṛśyate tv agryayā buddhyā sūkṣmayā sūkṣmadarśibhiḥ ||1.3.12||

word_meanings.meaning: by those who see the subtle

verses.english_translation: This Self, hidden in all beings, does not shine forth. But it is seen by those who see the subtle, through a sharp and subtle intellect.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: सर्वेषु भूतेषु गूढः — सर्वेषु प्राणिषु अन्तर्हितः। न प्रकाशते — सामान्यजनाय न दृश्यते। अग्र्यया बुद्ध्या — तीक्ष्णया बुद्ध्या — सूक्ष्मदर्शिभिः — दृश्यते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is hidden in all beings but does not reveal itself to ordinary perception. It is seen only by those with a sharp, subtle intellect — those who have purified their minds through discrimination and meditation.

verses.transliteration: yacched vāṅ manasī prājñas tad yacchej jñāna ātmani ||1.3.13||

word_meanings.meaning: let him restrain speech in mind

verses.english_translation: Let the wise restrain speech in mind, mind in the knowing Self.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: वाक् मनसि — वाचम् मनसि लयं नयेत्। मनः ज्ञाने — मनः बुद्धौ।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: This describes the yogic process of withdrawal. Speech is dissolved into mind, mind into intellect. This is the path of meditation leading to samadhi and the direct experience of the Self.

verses.sanskrit: एतद्ध्येवाक्षरं ब्रह्म एतद्ध्येवाक्षरं परम् | एतद्ध्येवाक्षरं ज्ञात्वा यो यदिच्छति तस्य तत् ||१.२.१६||
verses.transliteration: etad dhy evākṣaraṃ brahma etad dhy evākṣaraṃ param | etad dhy evākṣaraṃ jñātvā yo yad icchati tasya tat ||1.2.16||
verses.english_translation: This syllable is indeed Brahman; this syllable is indeed the supreme. Knowing this syllable, whatever one desires — that is his.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: एतत् अक्षरम् — ओंकारः — ब्रह्म — परम्। एतत् ज्ञात्वा — यः यत् इच्छति — तस्य तत् — सर्वम् प्राप्नोति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The syllable Om is Brahman itself — the supreme. Knowing Om as Brahman, whatever one desires is attained. For the highest seeker, the desire is liberation — and that too is attained through the knowledge of Om.

verses.sanskrit: सर्वे वेदा यत्पदमामनन्ति तपांसि सर्वाणि च यद्वदन्ति | यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति तत्ते पदं संग्रहेण ब्रवीम्योमित्येतत् ||१.२.१५||
verses.transliteration: sarve vedā yat padam āmananti tapāṃsi sarvāṇi ca yad vadanti | yad icchanto brahmacaryaṃ caranti tat te padaṃ saṃgraheṇa bravīmy om ity etat ||1.2.15||

word_meanings.meaning: which goal all the Vedas declare

verses.english_translation: The goal which all the Vedas declare, which all austerities proclaim, desiring which people practice celibacy — that goal I tell you briefly: it is Om.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: सर्वे वेदाः — यत् पदम् — आमनन्ति — ओंकारम्। तपांसि — यत् वदन्ति — ओंकारम्। ब्रह्मचर्यम् — यत् इच्छन्तः — चरन्ति — ओंकारम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: All the Vedas, all austerities, and all spiritual practices point to one goal — Om. Om is the symbol of Brahman, the ultimate reality. Yama reveals this to Nachiketa as the supreme secret.

verses.sanskrit: नचिकेतमग्निं तं विद्याद्वेदमेदं प्रतिष्ठितम् | अमृतत्वं च गच्छति य इमं ब्रह्मणो विदुः ||१.१.१९||
verses.transliteration: naciketam agniṃ taṃ vidyād vedam edaṃ pratiṣṭhitam | amṛtatvaṃ ca gacchati ya imaṃ brahmaṇo viduḥ ||1.1.19||
verses.english_translation: Know this as the Nachiketa fire, established in this knowledge. Those who know this Brahman attain immortality.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नचिकेतम् अग्निम् — नचिकेतसा पृष्टः अग्निः — नचिकेताग्निः। य इमम् ब्रह्मणः विदुः — ते अमृतत्वम् गच्छन्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Yama names the fire after Nachiketa — the Nachiketa fire. Those who know this fire as Brahman attain immortality. This is the fulfillment of Nachiketa's second boon — the knowledge of the fire that leads to heaven and ultimately to liberation.

verses.sanskrit: अन्यत्र धर्मादन्यत्राधर्मादन्यत्रास्मात्कृताकृतात् | अन्यत्र भूताच्च भव्याच्च यत्तत्पश्यसि तद्वद ||१.२.१४||
verses.transliteration: anyatra dharmād anyatrādharmād anyatrāsmāt kṛtākṛtāt | anyatra bhūtāc ca bhavyāc ca yat tat paśyasi tad vada ||1.2.14||

word_meanings.meaning: other than the done and undone

verses.english_translation: Other than dharma, other than adharma, other than the done and undone, other than past and future — what you see as that, tell me that.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अन्यत्र धर्मात् — धर्माधर्मयोः अतीतम्। कृताकृतात् अन्यत्र — कर्मणः अतीतम्। भूतभव्यात् अन्यत्र — कालात् अतीतम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa asks Yama to describe that which is beyond dharma and adharma, beyond action and its results, beyond past and future. This is the description of Brahman — the timeless, actionless, pure consciousness that transcends all categories.

verses.sanskrit: सर्वे वेदा यत्पदमामनन्ति | तपांसि सर्वाणि च यद्वदन्ति | यदिच्छन्तो ब्रह्मचर्यं चरन्ति | तत्ते पदं संग्रहेण ब्रवीमि | ओमित्येतत् ||१.२.१५||
verses.transliteration: sarve vedā yat padam āmananti | tapāṃsi sarvāṇi ca yad vadanti | yad icchanto brahmacaryaṃ caranti | tat te padaṃ saṃgraheṇa bravīmi | om ity etat ||1.2.15||
verses.english_translation: The goal which all the Vedas declare, which all austerities proclaim, desiring which people practice celibacy — that goal I tell you briefly: it is Om.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: ओंकारः — सर्ववेदप्रतिपाद्यः — सर्वतपःफलम् — ब्रह्मचर्यलक्ष्यम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Om is the goal of all the Vedas, all austerities, and all spiritual practices. It is the symbol of Brahman — the ultimate reality. Knowing Om as Brahman is the highest knowledge.

verses.sanskrit: पुरमेकादशद्वारमजस्यावक्रचेतसः | अनुष्ठाय न शोचति विमुक्तश्च विमुच्यते | एतद्वै तत् ||२.२.१||
verses.transliteration: puram ekādaśadvāram ajasyāvakracetasaḥ | anuṣṭhāya na śocati vimuktaś ca vimucyate | etad vai tat ||2.2.1||

word_meanings.meaning: meditating on it, one does not grieve

verses.english_translation: The city with eleven gates of the unborn, straight-minded one — meditating on it, one does not grieve. Liberated, one is freed. This is indeed That.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: पुरम् एकादशद्वारम् — शरीरम् — एकादश इन्द्रियद्वाराणि। अजस्य — आत्मनः — अवक्रचेतसः — ऋजुचित्तस्य।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The 'city with eleven gates' is the body — with nine openings (two eyes, two ears, two nostrils, mouth, navel, and the lower opening) plus the crown of the head and the heart. The unborn Self dwells in this city. Meditating on the Self in the body, one is freed from grief and attains liberation.

verses.sanskrit: न तत्र सूर्यो भाति न चन्द्रतारकं नेमा विद्युतो भान्ति कुतोऽयमग्निः | तमेव भान्तमनुभाति सर्वं तस्य भासा सर्वमिदं विभाति ||२.२.१५||
verses.transliteration: na tatra sūryo bhāti na candratārakaṃ nemā vidyuto bhānti kuto'yam agniḥ | tam eva bhāntam anubhāti sarvaṃ tasya bhāsā sarvam idaṃ vibhāti ||2.2.15||

word_meanings.meaning: the sun does not shine there

verses.english_translation: The sun does not shine there, nor the moon and stars, nor these lightnings — how then this fire? Everything shines after that one shines. By its light all this is illumined.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: तमेव भान्तम् — ब्रह्म एव प्रकाशमानम् — अनुभाति सर्वम् — सूर्यादयः अनुप्रकाशन्ते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Brahman alone is self-luminous. The sun, moon, stars, and fire all shine by reflecting Brahman's light. Without Brahman's consciousness illumining them, no light could shine. This verse appears also in the Mundaka and Bhagavad Gita.

verses.sanskrit: ऊर्ध्वमूलोऽवाक्शाख एषोऽश्वत्थः सनातनः | तदेव शुक्रं तद्ब्रह्म तदेवामृतमुच्यते | तस्मिँल्लोकाः श्रिताः सर्वे तदु नात्येति कश्चन | एतद्वै तत् ||२.३.१||
verses.transliteration: ūrdhvamūlo'vākśākha eṣo'śvatthaḥ sanātanaḥ | tad eva śukraṃ tad brahma tad evāmṛtam ucyate | tasmiṃl lokāḥ śritāḥ sarve tad u nātyeti kaścana | etad vai tat ||2.3.1||

word_meanings.meaning: that is the pure, that is Brahman

verses.english_translation: This eternal Ashvattha tree has its root above and branches below. That is the pure; that is Brahman; that is called immortality. In it all worlds rest; none goes beyond it. This is indeed That.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: ऊर्ध्वमूलः — ब्रह्म मूलम् — ऊर्ध्वे। अवाक्शाखः — जगत् शाखाः — अधः। अश्वत्थः — न श्वः स्थास्यति — अनित्यः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The eternal tree with roots above and branches below represents the universe with Brahman as its root. The root (Brahman) is above — transcendent; the branches (the world) spread downward. All worlds rest in Brahman. This image also appears in the Bhagavad Gita (15.1).
